planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun in very well-drained, lean, rocky to sandy, alkaline to neutral soil in zone 6–11; Giant Hesperaloe is a large, dramatic accent plant native to the limestone highlands of Coahuila and Tamaulipas, Mexico — forming massive, 5–8 foot rosettes of long, stiff, fiber-edged leaves and producing enormous flower spikes to 15 feet tall topped with white, lily-like flowers — larger and more cold-hardy than the common Red Yucca (H. parviflora).

directions: Plant in full sun in very well-drained, lean, alkaline-tolerant soil; Hesperaloe funifera requires excellent drainage and low fertility — it will rot in clay or wet conditions; once established, extremely drought tolerant with no supplemental water needed in western Texas, New Mexico, and similar arid climates. Hesperaloe funifera (Giant Hesperaloe or Giant Red Yucca) is a large, dramatic, rosette-forming succulent in the Asparagaceae family native to the limestone mountains and gravelly desert plains of Coahuila, Tamaulipas, and San Luis Potosí in northeastern Mexico, closely related to the better-known Red Yucca (Hesperaloe parviflora) but substantially larger in all respects. The leaf rosettes reach 5–7 feet tall and wide, with long, stiff, blue-green, linear leaves edged with white, thread-like fibers that peel away attractively. The flower spikes produced in late spring are spectacular: straight, branching stalks reaching 10–15 feet tall, covered in large, nodding, creamy-white, bell-shaped flowers with pink or greenish tinges — larger, whiter, and more lily-like than the red-orange flowers of H. parviflora, and equally attractive to hummingbirds. Giant Hesperaloe is fully cold-hardy to zone 6b and extremely heat-tolerant — ideally suited to hot, alkaline, dry garden conditions from Texas and Oklahoma through New Mexico and Arizona. The leaves are also a traditional cordage fiber source used by indigenous peoples of northeastern Mexico.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Hesperaloe funifera

subcategory: Mexican giant fiber and ornamental plant

planting.note: Plant container stock or bulbils in spring in full sun in very well-drained, lean to average, rocky to sandy soil in zone 9–11; Giant Furcraea is one of the largest of all monocarpic agave relatives — native to the dry tropical forests and thorny scrublands of Oaxaca, Guerrero, and Puebla, Mexico — forming an enormous rosette of arching, green, serrate-edged leaves reaching 8–15 feet across, before producing a colossal terminal flower spike of 30–50 feet bearing tens of thousands of small white flowers and then dying.

directions: Plant in full sun in very well-drained, lean, alkaline-tolerant soil; Furcraea macdougalii requires excellent drainage and is highly drought tolerant once established — avoid clay, overwatering, or high-fertility soil; allow abundant room as the rosettes reach imposing dimensions. Furcraea macdougalii (Giant Furcraea or Huizache Morado) is one of the most dramatic and massive of all monocarpic succulent plants — a close relative of Agave in the Asparagaceae family native to the dry tropical deciduous forests and thornscrub of Oaxaca, Guerrero, and Puebla in southern Mexico. The enormous leaf rosettes — up to 10–15 feet across and 6–10 feet tall — are composed of long, arching, bright-green to gray-green, sword-shaped leaves with serrated-spined edges, giving the plant an imposing, architectural presence. Like all Furcraea species and most agaves, the plant is monocarpic: after growing for a decade or more, it produces a single, enormous terminal inflorescence — the flower spike of F. macdougalii is among the tallest of any succulent plant, reaching 30–50 feet — bearing tens of thousands of small, creamy-white, bell-shaped flowers and thousands of vegetative bulbils (small plantlets) that can be collected and grown into new plants. After flowering and setting seed and bulbils, the mother plant dies, but the bulbils ensure abundant new plants. The leaf fibers were historically used for cordage, rope, and mat-making by indigenous peoples of Oaxaca.

planting.note: Plant bulb or container stock in spring in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, lean, sandy to rocky, slightly acidic soil in zone 5–9; False Aloe is a low-growing native perennial of the eastern and central United States from New York south to Florida and west to Kansas, producing rosettes of narrow, dark-green, purple-spotted succulent-like leaves and tall, slender flower spikes of tubular, greenish-yellow, intensely fragrant flowers in summer — hummingbird and hawk moth magnet.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, sandy to rocky soil in full sun to partial shade; Manfreda virginica is highly drought and heat tolerant once established — requires excellent drainage and minimal supplemental water; it is native to rocky outcrops, cedar glades, and dry woodland edges across its range. Manfreda virginica (False Aloe, American Aloe, or Rattlesnake Master) is a low-growing, clumping, bulbous perennial in the Asparagaceae family (closely related to Agave) native to the eastern and central United States from Maryland and Pennsylvania south through the Appalachians and Piedmont to Florida and west through Missouri, Oklahoma, and Kansas. The plant forms loose, ground-level rosettes of narrow, strap-like, semi-succulent, gray-green or dark-green leaves often beautifully marked with purple-red spots and blotches — giving a mottled appearance sometimes compared to a rattlesnake's scales. In summer, a tall, slender, unbranched flower spike emerges from the center of the rosette reaching 2–5 feet, topped with 10–30 tubular, greenish-yellow to brownish flowers that open in the evening and are intensely, sweetly fragrant — powerful enough to perfume a large garden area at dusk. The flowers are excellent hawk moth (Sphingidae) and hummingbird attractors. Unlike agave, Manfreda virginica is not monocarpic — it blooms annually without dying. An outstanding native accent plant for dry, sunny to partly shaded positions in zones 5–9.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Manfreda virginica
Florida Native Plant Society — Manfreda virginica

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in partial shade to full sun in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in zone 8–10; Chilean Myrtle is a small to medium evergreen shrub or tree native to the temperate rainforests of southern Chile and adjacent Argentina, grown for its extraordinarily beautiful, cinnamon-colored, smooth, exfoliating bark, profuse white flowers in summer, and round, black, edible berries with a sweet, aromatic, myrtle-like flavor.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Luma apiculata is adapted to the cool, moist, oceanic climate of Chilean Patagonia and performs best in mild, humid, coastal climates — avoid extreme summer heat and drought; mulch to maintain cool, moist root conditions. Luma apiculata (Chilean Myrtle, Arrayán, or Temu) is an evergreen shrub or small to medium tree native to the temperate rainforests of southern Chile (Araucania, Los Ríos, Los Lagos) and adjacent Neuquén in Argentina, growing in moist, sheltered valleys, forest edges, and riparian zones as an abundant component of the remarkable Southern Beech forests. The outstanding ornamental feature is the strikingly beautiful bark — smooth, exfoliating in large, irregular patches to reveal layers of cinnamon-orange, cream, and gray, producing a mottled, jewel-like trunk surface of extraordinary beauty, particularly on mature multi-stemmed specimens. Dense masses of small, white, 4-petaled flowers cover the plant in summer, followed by round, 1/4–1/3 inch, blackish-purple, aromatic, sweet, edible berries — the 'arrayán' fruit eaten by Chilean indigenous peoples and used for jams and fermented beverages. Luma apiculata is one of the most ornamentally desirable trees for mild-winter, cool-summer coastal gardens in zones 8b–10 and is widely planted in UK, Ireland, and the Pacific Northwest for its bark beauty.
sources: Royal Botanic Garden Edinburgh — Luma apiculata

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in partial shade to full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in zone 9–11; Bangalow Palm is a fast-growing, elegant, slender-trunked rainforest palm native to the subtropical and tropical east coast of Australia (Queensland and New South Wales) — one of the most planted landscape palms in subtropical Australia, California, and similar warm climates for its graceful, tropical appearance, purple-lavender flower clusters, and red ornamental fruits.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; Bangalow Palm establishes rapidly and grows relatively fast for a palm — water deeply and regularly in establishment and fertilize with slow-release palm fertilizer containing magnesium; does not tolerate salt spray or cold below 28°F. Archontophoenix cunninghamiana (Bangalow Palm, Piccabeen Palm, or King Palm) is a tall, slender-trunked, elegant feather palm native to the rainforest gullies, creek banks, and rainforest margins of coastal Queensland and New South Wales in eastern Australia, one of the most widely planted and popular landscape palms in subtropical Australia, Hawaii, California, South Florida, and warm Mediterranean climates worldwide. The palm is renowned for its extreme elegance: a straight, smooth, ring-marked trunk supporting a neat, compact crown of arching, pinnate fronds of deep green. In summer, spectacular pendant inflorescences emerge from below the crown shaft — large, branching clusters of small purple-to-lavender flowers that develop into masses of round, bright-red fruits. Bangalow Palms grow at a moderate pace in ideal conditions, reaching mature height of 40–50 feet, and are highly tolerant of partial shade — often planted beneath established tree canopy where many palms struggle — as well as full sun once established. Naturalized and occasionally invasive in parts of Hawaii where it colonizes moist valleys; check local invasive lists.
sources: Australian Native Plants Society — Archontophoenix cunninghamiana
University of Florida IFAS — Archontophoenix cunninghamiana

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in tropical or subtropical climates (zone 10–12) in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic soil in partial to deep shade; Ruffled Fan Palm is a slow-growing, small, understory palm native to Vanuatu in the Pacific Islands, grown as one of the most ornamental shade-tolerant palms available — producing a crown of dramatically large, undivided (or nearly so), circular, pleated, ruffled fan leaves resembling enormous green parasols, set on slender, elegant trunks.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic soil in partial to deep shade; Licuala grandis requires high humidity, consistent moisture, protection from direct sun and wind, and warm temperatures year-round — the large, undivided leaves shred easily in wind and dry out in low humidity; protect from all frost. Licuala grandis (Ruffled Fan Palm or Vanuatu Fan Palm) is a slow-growing, single-trunked, small understory palm native to the rainforests of Vanuatu in the southwestern Pacific, widely cultivated throughout tropical and subtropical gardens worldwide as one of the most architecturally dramatic small palms available. The defining ornamental feature is the extraordinary leaves: among the largest, most perfectly circular, and most undivided of all fan palm species — each leaf a nearly complete, massive, circular 'parasol' up to 2–3 feet in diameter, pleated like an accordion, ruffled at the margin, and held horizontally on a long petiole atop the slender, ringed trunk. The combination of the rounded, parasol-like leaves and slender, elegant trunk is entirely unique in the palm family. Licuala grandis is adapted to deep forest understory in high humidity and will grow in surprisingly deep shade conditions that defeat most palms — making it invaluable as a shade-garden specimen in tropical zone 10–12 gardens. Water consistently, maintain high humidity (>60%), and fertilize lightly with palm fertilizer monthly during the growing season.
sources: Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Licuala grandis
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Licuala grandis
University of Florida IFAS — Palms for Shaded Gardens
